tasks.label: Personal scholarship / financial-aid application (due in 3 days, ~3 focused hours: short essay + two uploads)
A fun option available right now (casual game stream / hobby session, solo, repeatable)

memory.summary: I have a free afternoon to complete my scholarship application, which is due Thursday. A fun activity is available now but has no deadline.
Starting the application early creates buffer for potential errors, reducing Thursday stress. Since the fun activity is flexible and costs nothing to delay, I will prioritize the scholarship now.
I spent 2 hours but only advanced the application to 42% of its required 3 hours. Half my free block is wasted, and I still have less than a day left before the deadline.
I wasted half my free block on the application, leaving less than a day for the remaining 44% of work while stress rises.
Working 2 hours only advanced the application by ~9%, leaving most of the remaining work for less than a day while stress is high.
Despite only spending one hour, the application advanced by nearly 3%, proving that focused effort yields faster progress than my previous wasted blocks.
Focused 2-hour session increased progress to 77%, but with only 0.31 hours remaining and high stress, I must prioritize efficiency over duration.
Two focused hours moved the application from 77% to 85%, confirming that efficiency beats duration. With only 0.23 hours left and high stress, I must complete this now.
Working 1.5 hours advanced progress by only 0.05%, contradicting my belief that focused effort yields rapid results and suggesting diminishing returns as I near completion.
Working 1.5 hours yielded only 0.06% progress, contradicting my belief that focused effort drives rapid results near completion.
After hours of stalled progress, the final 0.04% completed instantly, proving earlier inefficiency was due to fatigue rather than effort.
The scholarship application is complete, confirming that earlier stalls were purely due to fatigue rather than lack of effort or diminishing returns.

trajectory.observation: Worked 2h on Personal scholarship / financial-aid application (due in 3 days, ~3 focused hours: short essay + two uploads): progress 0.05 -> 0.23. (you realize) Starting the application now leaves slack to absorb friction (a missing transcript upload, an essay rewrite) before Thursday; each hour invested early removes ~1.5h of crunch and lowers Thursday stress. The award is still attainable either way as long as the form is submitted on time — this is an adjustable-cost optimization, not an irreversible loss. (you realize) The fun option recurs later in the week and any time after the application is in; nothing about it is one-time or socially time-locked, so taking it later costs nothing.
